title: practical english usage - Part 130
content:
There was none left.

emphatic.

Instead of not any without a noun, none (►   163)  can be used. This is often more ‘ Did you get the oil? ‘ No, there wasn't any left.' A  noun can be dropped after any, if the meaning is clear.

6 without      a  noun If any of your friends is/are interested, let me know.

plural. A  singular verb is more common in a formal style.

Note that when any of is followed by a plural subject, the verb can be singular or I don't think any of us want to work tomorrow.

- I don't think any staff want to work tomorrow.

Do any of these books belong to you?

- Do any books here belong to you?

I wasn't interested in any of the lectures, (     .. .-any-the lectures.) not - I didn't go to any lectures last term, (     ... any of lectures ...) pronoun, we  use any of. Compare: Before a determiner (definite article, demonstrative or possessive word) or a 5 any     and   any     of get any blue eyes - people have a definite number of eyes: two.) not Anna looks like her mother, but she hasn't got blue eyes, (     ... she hasn't - None of her children have got any sense. (Not even a small amount.) Birds have feathers, not hair. (No idea of amount.) - Dad hasn't got any hair. (He has lost the amount he had.) amount.) Is there water on the moon? (The interest is in the existence of water, not its - Is there any water in that can?

quantity or number, we  do not usually use any. Compare: indefinite but limited amount or number. When  there is no idea of a limited With an uncountable or plural noun, any usually suggests the idea of an 4 when     any     is  not   used not She hasn't got a Job. (     Ske-hasn'tget any Jeb.) We   do not usually use not any with singular countable nouns (but ►    160).

No tourists came to the town that year.

No cigarette is harmless, (     Not any cigarette...) Not any cannot begin a sentence; no is used instead.

grammar •  161   some and any: the main differences Have you got some glasses that I could borrow?

Could I have some brown rice, please?

Would you like some more meat?

Shouldn't there be some instructions with it?

Have you brought some paper and a pen? (The hearer is expected to encourage them to say 'Yes' - for example in offers and requests.

We   use some in questions if we  expect people to answer 'Yes', or want to 2  some      in  questions not Sorry, 1 don't have any flour, (     Sorry, I don't have some flour.) I need some flour. Do you have any flour?

affirmative' word (►   222) and is common in questions and negatives. Compare: Used in this sense, some is most common in affirmative clauses; any is a 'non­ I need some new clothes. Is there any milk left?

much/many we  are thinking of.

number. They  are used when it is not easy, or not important, to say exactly how Both some (►   158) and any (►    159) can refer to an indefinite quantity or 1  indefinite       quantities 161 some      and   any:    the   main    differences She doesn't speak any English at all.

I'll do any job at all - even road-sweeping.

At all (►  387) is often used to emphasise the meaning of (not) any.

I can write with either hand, (     ... any hand) alternatives.

Note that we  use either (►   156), not any, to talk about a choice between two work... could be misunderstood.) I don't do just any work -1 choose jobs that interest me. (I don't do any We can use just any if necessary to make the meaning clear.

'When should 1 come? 'Any time.’ My brother's looking for work. He'll take any job he can get.

Ask any doctor - they'll all tell you that alcohol is a poison.

it is stressed.

singular countable nouns as well as uncountables and plurals. In speech, affirmative clauses as well as questions and negatives, and is often used with 'it doesn't matter who/which/what'. With this meaning, any is common in Any can be used to emphasise the idea of free choice, with the meaning of 160 any     = 'it  doesn't     matter     who/which/what' Section Determiners:       Quantifiers              13 grammar •  162   any and every: the difference None of us speaks Greek.
